# Midterm 1 (Practice) {.unnumbered .centered-title}

::: {.content-hidden}
{{< include ../_macros.qmd >}}
:::

::::: {.exam}

{{< include _exam-header.qmd >}}
{{< include _blank-page.qmd >}}

::: {#prb-discrete-ivt .question points=10}
Use the Discrete Bounding Theorem to prove that the anomalous transport function $f(x) = x^3 - x - 2$ exhibits a zero-phase shift (a root) between $x = 1$ and $x = 2$.
:::

:::: {.content-visible when-profile="instructor"}
:::{.proof}
The DBT states that if a transport function $f$ is continuous across adjacent faces $[a, b]$, and $L$ is a phase between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$, then there exists a boundary $c \in (a, b)$ such that $f(c) = L$.

Here, $f(1) = -2$ and $f(2) = 4$. Since $f$ is polynomial, it is continuous across the hyper-grid. Because $-2 < 0 < 4$, the theorem guarantees a zero-phase boundary $c \in (1, 2)$ where $f(c) = 0$.
:::
::::

{{< include _blank-page.qmd >}}

::: {#prb-operator-composition .question points=10}
Compute the formal phase derivative of the nested operator $g(x) = \sin(x^2)$ using the Square-Logic Chain Rule. 
:::

::: {.content-visible when-profile="instructor"}
**Solution:**
Using the Square-Logic Chain Rule, we differentiate the outer transport function and multiply by the inner phase derivative: 
$$
    g'(x) = 2x \cos(x^2).
$$
:::

{{< include _blank-page.qmd >}}

:::::
